title: Youngstown, Ohio

infobox.value: US: 685th OH: [11th](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Ohio "List of municipalities in Ohio")

is a city in [Mahoning County, Ohio](/wiki/Mahoning_County,_Ohio "Mahoning County, Ohio"), United States, and the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") (however, a small portion of the city is in [Trumbull County](/wiki/Trumbull_County,_Ohio "Trumbull County, Ohio")). It lies along the [Mahoning River](/wiki/Mahoning_River "Mahoning River") in [Northeast Ohio](/wiki/Northeast_Ohio "Northeast Ohio"). The population was 60,068 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census") (estimated at 59,123 in 2024),[[6]](#cite_note-2020_Census_(City)-6)[[5]](#cite_note-USCensusEst2024-5) making it the [eleventh-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Ohio "List of municipalities in Ohio") in Ohio. The [Mahoning Valley](/wiki/Mahoning_Valley "Mahoning Valley") metropolitan area had an estimated 426,086 residents in 2024.

**Youngstown** is a city in [Mahoning County, Ohio](/wiki/Mahoning_County,_Ohio "Mahoning County, Ohio"), United States, and the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") (however, a small portion of the city is in [Trumbull County](/wiki/Trumbull_County,_Ohio "Trumbull County, Ohio")). It lies along the [Mahoning River](/wiki/Mahoning_River "Mahoning River") in [Northeast Ohio](/wiki/Northeast_Ohio "Northeast Ohio"). The population was 60,068 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census") (estimated at 59,123 in 2024),[[6]](#cite_note-2020_Census_(City)-6)[[5]](#cite_note-USCensusEst2024-5) making it the [eleventh-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Ohio "List of municipalities in Ohio") in Ohio. The [Mahoning Valley](/wiki/Mahoning_Valley "Mahoning Valley") metropolitan area had an estimated 426,086 residents in 2024.

Youngstown was named after pioneer [John Young](/wiki/John_Young_(pioneer) "John Young (pioneer)"), who founded the city in 1797 within the [Connecticut Western Reserve](/wiki/Connecticut_Western_Reserve "Connecticut Western Reserve") and established the first [sawmill](/wiki/Sawmill "Sawmill") and [gristmill](/wiki/Gristmill "Gristmill") along the Mahoning River. It was an early industrial city of the late 19th and early 20th centuries and became known as a center of [steel production](/wiki/Iron_and_steel_industry_in_the_United_States "Iron and steel industry in the United States"). However, Youngstown did not economically diversify like larger industrial cities, and with the [movement of steelmaking jobs offshore](/wiki/History_of_the_iron_and_steel_industry_in_the_United_States "History of the iron and steel industry in the United States") as the industry contracted in the 1970s, the city became exemplary of the [Rust Belt](/wiki/Rust_Belt "Rust Belt").

Revitalization efforts in the 21st century include the [Covelli Centre](/wiki/Covelli_Centre "Covelli Centre") and [Youngstown Foundation Amphitheatre](/wiki/Youngstown_Foundation_Amphitheatre "Youngstown Foundation Amphitheatre"), while other notable city institutions include the [Butler Institute of American Art](/wiki/Butler_Institute_of_American_Art "Butler Institute of American Art"), [Mill Creek Park](/wiki/Mill_Creek_Park "Mill Creek Park"), [Stambaugh Auditorium](/wiki/Stambaugh_Auditorium "Stambaugh Auditorium"), and [Youngstown State University](/wiki/Youngstown_State_University "Youngstown State University"). Youngstown is about 5 miles (8.0 km) west of the Ohio–[Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") border andmidway between [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland") (60 miles (97 km) northwest) and [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh") (60 miles (97 km) southeast).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Founding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Founding")]

Youngstown was named for New York native [John Young](/wiki/John_Young_(pioneer) "John Young (pioneer)"), who surveyed the area in 1796 and settled there soon afterward.[[9]](#cite_note-vind-yo-10-15-24-9) On February 9, 1797, Young purchased the township of 15,560 acres (6,300 ha) from the [Western Reserve Land Company](/wiki/Connecticut_Western_Reserve "Connecticut Western Reserve") for $16,085.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The 1797 establishment of Youngstown was officially recorded on August 19, 1802.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The area that includes present-day Youngstown was part of the [Connecticut Western Reserve](/wiki/Connecticut_Western_Reserve "Connecticut Western Reserve"), a section of the [Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory") that [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut") initially did not cede to the federal government.[[12]](#cite_note-officialoh-12)[[13]](#cite_note-up-13) Upon cession, Connecticut retained the [title](/wiki/Title_(property) "Title (property)") to the land in the Western Reserve, which it sold to the [Connecticut Land Company](/wiki/Connecticut_Land_Company "Connecticut Land Company") for $1,200,000.[[12]](#cite_note-officialoh-12)[[13]](#cite_note-up-13)[[14]](#cite_note-ohsub-14) While many of the area's early settlers came from Connecticut, Youngstown attracted many [Scots-Irish](/wiki/Scots-Irish_American "Scots-Irish American") settlers from neighboring [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania").[[15]](#cite_note-15) The first European Americans to settle permanently in the area were Pittsburgh native James Hillman and wife Catherine Dougherty.[[16]](#cite_note-16) By 1798, Youngstown was the home of several families who were concentrated near where Mill Creek meets the [Mahoning River](/wiki/Mahoning_River "Mahoning River").[[17]](#cite_note-Blue13-17) [Boardman Township](/wiki/Boardman_Township,_Ohio "Boardman Township, Ohio") was founded in 1798 by [Elijah Boardman](/wiki/Elijah_Boardman "Elijah Boardman"), a member of the Connecticut Land Company. Also founded in 1798 was [Austintown](/wiki/Austintown,_Ohio "Austintown, Ohio") by John McCollum who was a settler from [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

As the Western Reserve's population grew, the need for administrative districts became apparent. In 1800, territorial governor [Arthur St. Clair](/wiki/Arthur_St._Clair "Arthur St. Clair") established Trumbull County (named in honor of Connecticut Governor [Jonathan Trumbull](/wiki/Jonathan_Trumbull "Jonathan Trumbull")), and designated the smaller settlement of Warren as its administrative center, or [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat").[[19]](#cite_note-19) In 1813, Trumbull County was divided into townships, with Youngstown Township comprising much of what became Mahoning County.[[20]](#cite_note-20) The village of Youngstown was incorporated in 1848, and in 1867 Youngstown was chartered as a city. It became the county seat in 1876, when the administrative center of Mahoning County was moved from neighboring [Canfield](/wiki/Canfield,_Ohio "Canfield, Ohio").[[21]](#cite_note-21) Youngstown has been Mahoning County's county seat to this day.[[22]](#cite_note-NACo-22)

### Growth and industrialization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Growth and industrialization")]

The discovery of coal by the community in the early 19th century paved the way for the Youngstown area's inclusion on the network of the famed [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal"). The [Pennsylvania and Ohio Canal](/wiki/Pennsylvania_and_Ohio_Canal "Pennsylvania and Ohio Canal") Company was organized in 1835, and the canal was completed in 1840.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Local industrialist [David Tod](/wiki/David_Tod "David Tod"), who became Ohio governor during the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), persuaded [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie") steamboat owners that coal mined in the Mahoning Valley could fuel their vessels if canal transportation were available between Youngstown and [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland"). The railroad's arrival in 1856 smoothed the path for further economic growth.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Youngstown's industrial development changed the face of the Mahoning Valley. The community's burgeoning coal industry drew hundreds of [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_Americans "Welsh Americans"), [German](/wiki/German_Americans "German Americans"), and [Irish](/wiki/Irish_Americans "Irish Americans") immigrants. With the establishment of steel mills in the late 19th century, Youngstown became a popular destination for Eastern European, [Italian](/wiki/Italian_Americans "Italian Americans"), and [Greek](/wiki/Greek_Americans "Greek Americans") immigrants.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/RepublicIron%26SteelWorks_YoungstownOH_1900s.jpg/250px-RepublicIron%26SteelWorks_YoungstownOH_1900s.jpg)](/wiki/File:RepublicIron%26SteelWorks_YoungstownOH_1900s.jpg)

[Republic Steel](/wiki/Republic_Steel "Republic Steel") works c. early 1900s

In the early 20th century, the community saw an influx of immigrants from non-European countries including what is modern day [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon"), [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"), [Palestine](/wiki/Palestine_(region) "Palestine (region)"), and [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria").[[26]](#cite_note-26) By the 1920s, this dramatic demographic shift produced a nativist backlash, and the Mahoning Valley became a center of [Ku Klux Klan](/wiki/Ku_Klux_Klan "Ku Klux Klan") activity.[[27]](#cite_note-27) The situation reached a climax in 1924, when street clashes between Klan members and [Italian](/wiki/Italian_American "Italian American") and [Irish](/wiki/Irish_American "Irish American") Americans in neighboring [Niles](/wiki/Niles,_Ohio "Niles, Ohio") led Ohio Governor [A. Victor Donahey](/wiki/A._Victor_Donahey "A. Victor Donahey") to declare martial law.[[28]](#cite_note-28) By 1928 the Klan was in steep decline; and three years later, the organization sold its Canfield, Ohio, meeting area, Kountry Klub Field.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Despite the prevalence of Irish Americans in Youngstown, their presence was not always evident. When radio personality Pete Gabriel (who was Greek) came to Youngstown, he found out that there was no [St. Patrick's Day](/wiki/St._Patrick%27s_Day "St. Patrick's Day") parade there at the time, so he started one.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

The growth of industry attracted people from within the United States and from [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America"). By the late 19th century, [African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans") were well represented in Youngstown, and the first local congregation of the [African Methodist Episcopal Church](/wiki/African_Methodist_Episcopal_Church "African Methodist Episcopal Church") was established in 1871.[[31]](#cite_note-Aley46-31) In the 1880s, local attorney [William R. Stewart](/wiki/William_R._Stewart "William R. Stewart") was the second African American elected to the [Ohio House of Representatives](/wiki/Ohio_House_of_Representatives "Ohio House of Representatives").[[32]](#cite_note-32) A large influx of African Americans in the early 20th century owed much to developments in the industrial sector. During the national [Steel Strike of 1919](/wiki/Steel_Strike_of_1919 "Steel Strike of 1919"), local industrialists recruited thousands of workers from the southern United States, many of whom were Black.[[33]](#cite_note-33) This move inflamed racist sentiment among local Whites, and for decades, African-American steelworkers experienced discrimination in the workplace.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35) Migration from the South rose dramatically in the 1940s, when the mechanization of southern agriculture brought an end to the [sharecropping](/wiki/Sharecropping "Sharecropping") system, leading onetime farm laborers to seek industrial jobs.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/YoungstownOhio1910s.jpg/250px-YoungstownOhio1910s.jpg)](/wiki/File:YoungstownOhio1910s.jpg)

Central Square and Viaduct in the 1910s (view looking south)

Youngstown's local [iron ore](/wiki/Iron_ore "Iron ore") deposits were exhausted by the early 20th century. Since the city is landlocked (the Mahoning River is not navigable), ore from [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan") and [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota") had to arrive by rail from Cleveland and other Great Lakes port cities where large bulk carriers were unloaded. This put Youngstown at a competitive disadvantage to the iron and steel producers in Cleveland, [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"), [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") and [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit")—all on Great Lake shores. Compared to these four cities, Youngstown had a higher cost of transporting raw materials to the mills, according to a *[Harvard Business Review](/wiki/Harvard_Business_Review "Harvard Business Review")* report published in January 1933. Higher transportation costs are one reason why Youngstown mills began their decline slightly earlier than manufacturing in other cities.[[37]](#cite_note-Transport_costs-37)

The city had a healthy position within the midwestern United States in terms of transportation connections. An airport built in 1930 hosted [Capital](/wiki/Capital_Airlines_(United_States) "Capital Airlines (United States)") and [United Airlines](/wiki/United_Airlines "United Airlines") flights through the region and to New York prior to the jet age of the latter 1950s. It was on the [Baltimore and Ohio Railroad](/wiki/Baltimore_and_Ohio_Railroad "Baltimore and Ohio Railroad") mainline to Chicago with the *[Capital Limited](/wiki/Capitol_Limited_(B%26O_train) "Capitol Limited (B&O train)")*. Likewise, Youngstown was on the [Erie Railroad](/wiki/Erie_Railroad "Erie Railroad") mainline, on its Chicago–Jersey City circuit, with trains such as the *[Atlantic Express/Pacific Express](/wiki/Atlantic_Express_and_Pacific_Express "Atlantic Express and Pacific Express")* and the *[Lake Cities](/wiki/Lake_Cities_(Erie_Railroad_train) "Lake Cities (Erie Railroad train)").* The city was on the [New York Central](/wiki/New_York_Central "New York Central")'s Pittsburgh–Buffalo circuit and the [Pennsylvania Railroad](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Railroad "Pennsylvania Railroad")'s Pittsburgh–Cleveland circuit.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)

### Post-World War II decline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Post-World War II decline")]

The city's population became more diverse after the end of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), when a seemingly robust steel industry attracted thousands of workers.[[41]](#cite_note-41) In the 1950s, the Latino population grew significantly and by the 1970s, St. Rose of Lima Catholic Church and the First Spanish Baptist Church of Ohio were among the largest religious institutions for Spanish-speaking residents in the Youngstown metropolitan area.[[31]](#cite_note-Aley46-31) In 1951, city planners projected that Youngstown would grow to 200,000 to 250,000 in population due to continuously strong demand for domestic steel in [western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), and [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea"), and so 12,000 acres on the city's East Side were annexed and extended utilities in expectation of future housing projects, in addition to aggressive re-zoning for expanded commercial spaces throughout the city.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Youngstown_Sheet%26Tube_Abandoned.jpg/250px-Youngstown_Sheet%26Tube_Abandoned.jpg)](/wiki/File:Youngstown_Sheet%26Tube_Abandoned.jpg)

A now-demolished Youngstown Sheet and Tube Company plant in 2006

A [strike action](/wiki/Strike_action "Strike action") occurred on September 6, 1967, when only 9 of the 50 scheduled patrolmen reported for duty at the Youngstown Police Department. Instead, the patrolmen, eventually numbering around 300, along with approximately 300 city-employed firefighters, were attending "continuous professional meetings." They vowed to continue doing so until their demand for an immediate across-the-board pay raise of $1,200 was met.[[43]](#cite_note-43) By September 9, when a county judge ordered them back to work, citizens were reportedly disturbed by the risks posed by police and fire services operating at roughly 30% of normal staffing levels. The most serious incident during the walkout was a car fire. When the judge ended the work stoppage, he also ordered the pay raise.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Apart from a fruitless six-day "sick call" of police in Detroit in June 1967, Youngstown's action was the first major police strike since the [Boston police strike](/wiki/Boston_police_strike "Boston police strike") in 1919. *[The Sheboygan Press](/wiki/The_Sheboygan_Press "The Sheboygan Press")* of [Sheboygan, Wisconsin](/wiki/Sheboygan,_Wisconsin "Sheboygan, Wisconsin"), observed, "So we have seen the first successful strike by policemen and firemen. It is a precedent over which there should be little rejoicing."[[45]](#cite_note-45)

The [industrial economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Youngstown,_Ohio "Economy of Youngstown, Ohio") that drew various groups to the area collapsed in the late 1970s, culminating with the September 19, 1977, closure of the [Youngstown Sheet and Tube](/wiki/Youngstown_Sheet_and_Tube "Youngstown Sheet and Tube") Campbell works after financial downturn due to changes in the steel manufacturing process and international competition.[[46]](#cite_note-46) In 1979–1980, [U.S. Steel](/wiki/U.S._Steel "U.S. Steel") pulled out of the Youngstown area, and in the mid-1980s, Republic Steel also filed for bankruptcy.[[47]](#cite_note-Bruno_1999,_pp._9–10-47) Attempts to revive the local steel industry proved unsuccessful. Shortly after the closure of most of Youngstown Sheet and Tube's area operations, local religious leaders, steelworkers, and activists such as [Staughton Lynd](/wiki/Staughton_Lynd "Staughton Lynd") participated in a grassroots effort to purchase and refurbish the company's abandoned plant in neighboring [Campbell, Ohio](/wiki/Campbell,_Ohio "Campbell, Ohio").[[48]](#cite_note-Fuechtmann5-48) In response to subsequent challenges, the city has taken well-publicized steps to diversify economically, while building on some traditional strengths.[[49]](#cite_note-nasser-49)

### Modern developments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Modern developments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Nathaniel_R._Jones_Federal_Building_%26_U.S._Courthouse.jpg/250px-Nathaniel_R._Jones_Federal_Building_%26_U.S._Courthouse.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nathaniel_R._Jones_Federal_Building_%26_U.S._Courthouse.jpg)

The [Nathaniel R. Jones Federal Building and U.S. Courthouse](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_Northern_District_of_Ohio "United States District Court for the Northern District of Ohio") was built during an era of new downtown construction in the early 2000s.

Downtown Youngstown has seen modest levels of new construction. In the 2000s, additions included the [Nathaniel R. Jones Federal Building and U.S. Courthouse](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_Northern_District_of_Ohio "United States District Court for the Northern District of Ohio") in 2002, named for Youngstown native [Nathaniel R. Jones](/wiki/Nathaniel_R._Jones "Nathaniel R. Jones") and designed by [Robert A. M. Stern](/wiki/Robert_A._M._Stern "Robert A. M. Stern") Architects,[[50]](#cite_note-50) the Mahoning County Children's Services Center and [George Voinovich](/wiki/George_Voinovich "George Voinovich") Government Center in 2004, and both the [Covelli Centre](/wiki/Covelli_Centre "Covelli Centre") and [Ohio Seventh District Court of Appeals](/wiki/Ohio_Seventh_District_Court_of_Appeals "Ohio Seventh District Court of Appeals") in 2006. The Covelli Centre was funded primarily through a $26 million federal grant secured in 2000 by then-Congressman [James Traficant](/wiki/James_Traficant "James Traficant") and is located on the site of a former steel mill downtown.[[51]](#cite_note-skolnick3-51) The arena's main tenants are the [Youngstown Phantoms](/wiki/Youngstown_Phantoms "Youngstown Phantoms") junior hockey team.

In 2004, construction began on a 60-home upscale development called Arlington Heights, and a grant from the [United States Department of Housing and Urban Development](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Housing_and_Urban_Development "United States Department of Housing and Urban Development") allowed for the demolition of Westlake Terrace, a sprawling and dilapidated public housing project. Today, the site features a blend of senior housing, rental townhouses and for-sale single-family homes. Low real-estate prices and the efforts of the Youngstown Central Area Improvement Corporation have contributed to the purchase of several long-abandoned downtown buildings (many by outside investors) and their restoration and conversion into specialty shops, restaurants, and eventually condominiums. In addition, a $250 million [New Urbanist](/wiki/New_Urbanist "New Urbanist") revitalization of the Smoky Hollow neighborhood developed about 400 new residential units, university student housing, retail space, and a park.[[52]](#cite_note-skolnick2-52)

In 2005, Federal Street, a major downtown thoroughfare that was closed off to create a pedestrian-oriented plaza, reopened to traffic. The downtown area has seen the razing of structurally unsound buildings and the expansion or restoration of others.[[53]](#cite_note-vindy-yo-09-21-06-53) New construction has dovetailed with efforts to cultivate business growth. One of the area's more successful business ventures in recent years has been the Youngstown [Business Incubator](/wiki/Business_Incubator "Business Incubator"), which fosters the growth of fledgling technology-based companies.[[54]](#cite_note-shilling-54)

In line with these efforts to change the community's image, the city government, in partnership with Youngstown State University, has organized an ambitious urban renewal plan known as *Youngstown 2010*. The stated goals of *Youngstown 2010* include the creation of a "cleaner, greener, and better planned and organized Youngstown". In January 2005, the organization unveiled a master plan prepared by Urban Strategies Inc. of [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"), which had taken shape during an extensive process of public consultation and meetings that gathered input from citizens.[[55]](#cite_note-schatz-55) The plan, which included platforms such as the acceptance of a reduced population and an improved image and quality of life, received national attention and is consistent with efforts in other metropolitan areas to address the phenomenon of urban depopulation.[[49]](#cite_note-nasser-49) *Youngstown 2010* received an award for public outreach from the [American Planning Association](/wiki/American_Planning_Association "American Planning Association") in 2007.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

Youngstown's first new downtown hotel since 1974—the [DoubleTree by Hilton](/wiki/DoubleTree_by_Hilton "DoubleTree by Hilton")—opened in 2018 in the historic Stambaugh Building, adapted for this use.[[57]](#cite_note-57) On May 28, 2024, [an explosion](/wiki/Realty_Building_explosion "Realty Building explosion") destroyed most of the first floor of the Realty Building in downtown Youngstown and severely damaged the floors above it, killing one bank employee and injuring seven.[[58]](#cite_note-58) The explosion was suspected to have been caused by a [natural gas leak](/wiki/Methane_leak "Methane leak").

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Mahoning_River_Market_Street_Bridge_Youngstown.jpg/250px-Mahoning_River_Market_Street_Bridge_Youngstown.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mahoning_River_Market_Street_Bridge_Youngstown.jpg)

The [Mahoning River](/wiki/Mahoning_River "Mahoning River") in Youngstown

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city has a total area of 34.564 square miles (89.52 km2), of which 33.929 square miles (87.88 km2) is land and 0.635 square miles (1.64 km2) (1.84%) is water.[[4]](#cite_note-CenPopGazetteer2025-4)

Youngstown is in the [geographic Mahoning Valley](/wiki/Mahoning_Valley_(geographic) "Mahoning Valley (geographic)") on the [Glaciated Allegheny Plateau](/wiki/Glaciated_Allegheny_Plateau "Glaciated Allegheny Plateau"). At the end of the [last ice age](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Period "Last Glacial Period"), the [glaciers](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier") left behind a uniform plain, with valleys such as that caused by the [Mahoning River](/wiki/Mahoning_River "Mahoning River") traversing the plain.[[59]](#cite_note-Aley89-59) Lakes created by glaciers that dammed small streams were eventually drained, leaving behind fertile terrain.[[59]](#cite_note-Aley89-59)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Climate")]

Youngstown has a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Dfb*/*Dfa*), typical of the [Midwestern United States](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States"), with four distinct seasons and lies within USDA [hardiness zone](/wiki/Hardiness_zone "Hardiness zone") 6a.[[60]](#cite_note-60) Winters are cold and dry but typically bring a mix of rain, sleet, and snow with occasional heavy snowfall and icing. January is the coldest month with an average mean temperature of 26.8 °F (−2.9 °C),[[61]](#cite_note-NOAA-61) with temperatures on average dropping to or below 0 °F (−18 °C) on 4.1 days and staying at or below freezing on 43 days per year.[[61]](#cite_note-NOAA-61) Snowfall averages 67.8 inches (172 cm) per season, somewhat less than the [snowbelt](/wiki/Snowbelt "Snowbelt") areas closer to [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie").[[61]](#cite_note-NOAA-61) The snowiest month on record was 53.1 inches (135 cm) in December 2010, while winter snowfall amounts have ranged from 118.7 in (301 cm) in 2010–11 to 25.2 in (64 cm) in 1948–49.[[61]](#cite_note-NOAA-61) Springs generally see a transition to fewer weather systems that produce heavier rainfall. Summers are typically very warm and humid with temperatures exceeding 90 °F (32 °C) on 7.7 days per year on average; the annual count has been as high as 40 days in 1943, while the most recent year to not reach that mark is 2014.[[61]](#cite_note-NOAA-61) July is the warmest month with an average mean temperature of 71.5 °F (22 °C).[[61]](#cite_note-NOAA-61)

The all-time record high temperature in Youngstown of 103 °F (39 °C) was established on July 10, 1936, which occurred during the [Dust Bowl](/wiki/Dust_Bowl "Dust Bowl"), and the all-time record low temperature of −22 °F (−30 °C) was set on January 19, 1994.[[61]](#cite_note-NOAA-61) The first and last freezes of the season on average fall on October 14 and May 6, respectively, allowing a growing season of 160 days; however, freezing temperatures have been observed in every month except July.[[61]](#cite_note-NOAA-61) The normal annual mean temperature is 49.9 °F (9.9 °C).[[61]](#cite_note-NOAA-61) Normal yearly [precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation_(meteorology) "Precipitation (meteorology)") based on the 30-year average from 1991 to 2020 is 41.19 inches (1,046 mm), falling on an average of 168 days per year.[[61]](#cite_note-NOAA-61) Monthly precipitation has ranged from 10.66 in (271 mm) in June 1986 to 0.16 in (4.1 mm) in October 1924, while for annual precipitation the historical range is 54.01 in (1,372 mm) in 2011 to 23.79 in (604 mm) in 1963.[[61]](#cite_note-NOAA-61)

### Neighborhoods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Neighborhoods")]

[Downtown Youngstown](/wiki/Downtown_Youngstown "Downtown Youngstown") consists of the original city layout designed by [John Young](/wiki/John_Young_(pioneer) "John Young (pioneer)") and contains government buildings, banks and entertainment venues, including the [Covelli Centre](/wiki/Covelli_Centre "Covelli Centre"), [Powers Auditorium](/wiki/Powers_Auditorium "Powers Auditorium") and [Youngstown Foundation Amphitheatre](/wiki/Youngstown_Foundation_Amphitheatre "Youngstown Foundation Amphitheatre"). North of downtown is [Youngstown State University](/wiki/Youngstown_State_University "Youngstown State University") and the [Butler Institute of American Art](/wiki/Butler_Institute_of_American_Art "Butler Institute of American Art"). The [Arlington](/wiki/Arlington_Avenue_Historic_District "Arlington Avenue Historic District"), Mahoning Commons, Riverbend, [Smoky Hollow](/wiki/Smoky_Hollow_(neighborhood) "Smoky Hollow (neighborhood)") and University neighborhoods surround downtown. The area is the least populous division of the city, but also its least residential by zoning. It is surrounded by the [I-680](/wiki/Interstate_680_(Ohio) "Interstate 680 (Ohio)") and Madison Avenue Expressway highway system.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/George_Renner_House.jpg/250px-George_Renner_House.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_Renner_House.jpg)

[George J. Renner Jr. House](/wiki/George_J._Renner_Jr._House "George J. Renner Jr. House"), part of the [Wick Park Historic District](/wiki/Wick_Park_Historic_District "Wick Park Historic District"). During the 20th century, Wick Park was home to the city's wealthiest families and mansions.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

The North Side consists of the [Brier Hill](/wiki/Brier_Hill "Brier Hill"), Crandall Park, [North Heights](/wiki/North_Heights "North Heights"), and [Wick Park](/wiki/Wick_Park_Historic_District "Wick Park Historic District") neighborhoods and the northern section of the Riverbend Industrial Park. Brier Hill was considered one of the city's cultural hotbeds due to many [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_Americans "Welsh Americans"), [Irish](/wiki/Irish_Americans "Irish Americans"), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_Americans "Italian Americans"), and [African American](/wiki/African_American "African American") migrants settling in it, but primarily was once viewed as the city's "[Little Italy](/wiki/Little_Italy "Little Italy")" as reflected by the [Brier Hill-style pizza](/wiki/Brier_Hill#Pizza "Brier Hill").[[67]](#cite_note-67) Each year, at the end of August, the Brier Hill Fest attracts thousands of visitors from [Northeast Ohio](/wiki/Northeast_Ohio "Northeast Ohio") and [Western Pennsylvania](/wiki/Western_Pennsylvania "Western Pennsylvania").[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrolio2004203-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70) The historic Crandall Park neighborhood was once home to the city's wealthiest families, and many of the mansions of industrial executives are still including in the Crandall Park-Fifth Avenue Historic District.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

Youngstown's South Side is, and historically has been, the city's densest and most populous division, with numerous neighborhoods from each of the city's periods of expansion. The older neighborhoods in this area, namely Oak Hill, Erie, [Warren](/wiki/Fosterville "Fosterville"), and Lower Gibson, were among the earliest settled outside of Youngstown during the 19th century and were annexed by the city by 1910. Later neighborhoods such as [Fosterville](/wiki/Fosterville "Fosterville"), Newport, [Lansingville](/wiki/Lansingville "Lansingville"), Buckeye Plat, and Cottage Grove came into being as industry and population expanded throughout the first half of the 20th century, being annexed in 1929 from the remainder of Youngstown Township. The Pleasant Grove and [Brownlee Woods](/wiki/Brownlee_Woods "Brownlee Woods") neighborhoods further south were also annexed in 1929 from [Boardman Township](/wiki/Boardman_Township,_Ohio "Boardman Township, Ohio"). The South Side shares [Mill Creek Park](/wiki/Mill_Creek_Park "Mill Creek Park") with the West Side.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

The East Side is the largest of the city's regions by area and consists of the East High, East Side, [Hazelton](/wiki/Hazelton_(Youngstown,_Ohio) "Hazelton (Youngstown, Ohio)"), Landsdowne, Lincoln Knolls, Scienceville and Sharon Line/McGuffey Heights communities. The neighborhoods on the East Side closest to downtown Youngstown were among the earliest developed in the city. However, much of the East Side is undeveloped land annexed in the 1950s as part of a zoning effort for future development that never occurred.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

The neighborhoods of Belle Vista, Cornersburg, Garden District, Kirkmere, Rocky Ridge, and [Schenley](/wiki/Schenley "Schenley") on the West Side were built from the 1930s until the 1950s progressing southward, apart from the early Steelton neighborhood and industrial Salt Springs neighborhood. The West Side shares [Mill Creek Park](/wiki/Mill_Creek_Park "Mill Creek Park") with the South Side and lies southwest of the Mahoning River.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

### 2020 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2020 census")]

As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), there were 60,068 people, 24,852 households, and 13,072 families residing in the city. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 1,770.40 inhabitants per square mile (683.6/km2). There were 28,675 housing units at an average density of 845.15 per square mile (326.3/km2). The median household income was $30,129.[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-82)

The median age was 38.5 years. 22.0% of residents were under the age of 18 and 17.7% of residents were 65 years of age or older. For every 100 females there were 97.8 males, and for every 100 females age 18 and over there were 96.0 males age 18 and over.[[82]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-82)

99.3% of residents lived in urban areas, while 0.7% lived in rural areas.[[83]](#cite_note-Census2020DHC-83)

There were 24,852 households in Youngstown, of which 25.4% had children under the age of 18 living in them. Of all households, 22.0% were married-couple households, 26.9% were households with a male householder and no spouse or partner present, and 43.4% were households with a female householder and no spouse or partner present. About 41.4% of all households were made up of individuals and 17.0% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older.[[82]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-82)

There were 28,675 housing units, of which 13.3% were vacant. The homeowner vacancy rate was 1.8% and the rental vacancy rate was 10.7%.[[82]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-82)

### 2010 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 2010 census")]

As of the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census"), there were 66,982 people, 26,839 households and 15,150 families residing in the city. The population density was 1,972.38 inhabitants per square mile (761.5/km2). There were 33,123 housing units at an average density of 975.35 per square mile (376.6/km2). The racial makeup of the city was 47.04% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 45.17% [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)"), 0.35% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 0.44% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.03% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Race_(U.S._Census) "Race (U.S. Census)"), 3.27% from some other races and 3.69% from two or more races. [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") people of any race were 9.27% of the population.

Youngstown's vacant-housing rate in 2010 was twenty times the national average.[[85]](#cite_note-Tavernise-85)

The European ancestry included had 10.8% [Italian](/wiki/Italian_American "Italian American"), 10.8% [Irish](/wiki/Irish_American "Irish American"), 10.0% [German](/wiki/German_American "German American"), and 4.2% [English](/wiki/English_American "English American") ancestries. Among the Hispanic population, 5.7% were [Puerto Rican](/wiki/Puerto_Rican_people "Puerto Rican people"), 1.9% [Mexican](/wiki/Mexican_American "Mexican American"), 0.1% [Cuban](/wiki/Cuban_American "Cuban American"), and 0.7% some other Hispanic or Latino.

Records suggest 28.6% of the households had children under the age of 18. Of these, 25.6% were [married couples](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") living together, 24.8% had a female householder with no husband present, and 43.6% were non-families. Meanwhile, 37.8% of all households comprised a single person, and 14.5% of households comprised a person over 65 years of age living alone. The average household size was 2.28 and the average family size was 3.02.

22.8% of the city's population was under the age of 18, 10.8% was from age 18 to 24, 24.3% was from age 25 to 44, 26.2% was from age 45 to 64, and 15.8% was age 65 or older. The median age was 38 years old. For every 100 females, there were 96.9 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 95 males.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of Youngstown, Ohio](/wiki/Economy_of_Youngstown,_Ohio "Economy of Youngstown, Ohio")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Downtown_Youngstown_in_2020.jpg/250px-Downtown_Youngstown_in_2020.jpg)](/wiki/File:Downtown_Youngstown_in_2020.jpg)

Federal Street in downtown Youngstown, with the [Metropolitan Tower](/wiki/Metropolitan_Tower_(Youngstown) "Metropolitan Tower (Youngstown)") on the left

The city's largest employer is [Youngstown State University](/wiki/Youngstown_State_University "Youngstown State University"), a public, urban university that serves about 11,000 students, just north of downtown.[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-ysu-communities-87)[[88]](#cite_note-headcount-88)

Youngstown is the site of several steel and metalworking operations, though not to the extent of the past. The largest industrial employers within the city limits are [Vallourec](/wiki/Vallourec "Vallourec") Star Steel Company, in the Brier Hill district, and Trivium Packaging.[[89]](#cite_note-89) [Steelite](/wiki/Steelite "Steelite"), a British ceramics manufacturer, has its U.S. headquarters based in Youngstown.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

The Youngstown [Business Incubator](/wiki/Business_incubator "Business incubator") (YBI), in the heart of downtown, houses several start-up technology companies that have received office space, furnishings, and access to utilities.[[54]](#cite_note-shilling-54) Some Incubator-supported companies have earned recognition, and a few are starting to outgrow their current space. *[Inc. Magazine](/wiki/Inc._Magazine "Inc. Magazine")* rated one such company–[Turning Technologies](/wiki/Turning_Technologies "Turning Technologies")–as the fastest-growing privately held software company in the United States and 18th fastest-growing privately held company overall.[[91]](#cite_note-91) To keep such companies downtown, the YBI secured approval to demolish a row of nearby vacant buildings to clear space for expansion.[[54]](#cite_note-shilling-54) In 2014, the YBI was ranked as the top university-associated business incubator in the world by the Swedish University Business Incubator Index.[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94) In 2015, the YBI was the top University Associated Incubator in North America, and came in second to the Guinness Enterprise Centre in [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin").[[95]](#cite_note-95)

A number of products and enterprises introduced in Youngstown became national household names. Among them is Youngstown-based [Schwebel's Bakery](/wiki/Schwebel%27s_Bakery "Schwebel's Bakery"), which was established in neighboring Campbell in the 20th century. The company now distributes bread products nationally.[[96]](#cite_note-96) In the 1920s, Youngstown was the birthplace of the [Good Humor](/wiki/Good_Humor "Good Humor") brand of ice cream novelties,[[97]](#cite_note-97) and the popular franchise of [Handel's Homemade Ice Cream & Yogurt](/wiki/Handel%27s_Homemade_Ice_Cream_%26_Yogurt "Handel's Homemade Ice Cream & Yogurt") was established there in the 1940s. In the 1950s, Youngstown-born developer [Edward J. DeBartolo Sr.](/wiki/Edward_J._DeBartolo_Sr. "Edward J. DeBartolo Sr.") established one of the country's first modern shopping plazas in the suburban Boardman.[[98]](#cite_note-98) The [fast-food](/wiki/Fast-food "Fast-food") chain, [Arby's](/wiki/Arby%27s "Arby's"), opened the first of its restaurants in Boardman in 1964, and [Arthur Treacher's Fish & Chips](/wiki/Arthur_Treacher%27s "Arthur Treacher's") was headquartered in Youngstown in the late 1970s. More recently, the city's downtown hosted the corporate headquarters of the now-defunct pharmacy chain store [Phar-Mor](/wiki/Phar-Mor "Phar-Mor"), which was established by Youngstown native [Mickey Monus](/wiki/Michael_I._Monus "Michael I. Monus").[[99]](#cite_note-newsweek-99)

### Historical

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Historical")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Blast_Furnace_and_Power_Plant%2C_1918_National_Archives_%2840089361451%29.jpg/250px-Blast_Furnace_and_Power_Plant%2C_1918_National_Archives_%2840089361451%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Blast_Furnace_and_Power_Plant,_1918_National_Archives_(40089361451).jpg)

[Youngstown Sheet and Tube](/wiki/Youngstown_Sheet_and_Tube "Youngstown Sheet and Tube") works in 1918

Endowed with large deposits of coal and iron as well as "old growth" hardwood forests needed to produce charcoal, the Youngstown area developed a thriving steel industry, starting with the area's blast furnace in 1803 by James and Daniel Heaton.[[100]](#cite_note-100) By the mid-19th century, Youngstown was the site of several iron industrial plants, and because of easy rail connections to adjacent states, the iron industry continued to expand in the 1890s despite the depletion of local natural resources.[[101]](#cite_note-101) At the turn of the 20th century, local industrialists began to convert to steel manufacturing, amid a wave of industrial consolidations that placed much of the Mahoning Valley's industry in the hands of national corporations.[[102]](#cite_note-Blue94-102)

In the late 1930s, the community's steel sector again gained national attention when Youngstown became a site of the so-called "Little Steel Strike", an effort by the [Steel Workers Organizing Committee](/wiki/Steel_Workers_Organizing_Committee "Steel Workers Organizing Committee"), a precursor to [United Steelworkers](/wiki/United_Steelworkers "United Steelworkers"), to secure contract agreements with smaller steel companies.[[103]](#cite_note-shellock-103) On June 21, 1937, strike-related violence in Youngstown resulted in two deaths and 42 injuries.[[103]](#cite_note-shellock-103) Despite the violence, historian William Lawson observed that the strike transformed industrial unions from "basically local and ineffective organizations into all-encompassing, nationwide collective bargaining representatives of American workers".[[103]](#cite_note-shellock-103)

Between the 1920s and 1960s, the city was known as an important industrial hub that featured the massive furnaces and foundries of such companies as [Republic Steel](/wiki/Republic_Steel "Republic Steel") and [U.S. Steel](/wiki/U.S._Steel "U.S. Steel"). At the same time, Youngstown never became economically diversified, as did larger industrial cities such as Chicago, Pittsburgh, Akron, or Cleveland.[[104]](#cite_note-Fuechtmann16-104) Hence, when economic changes forced the closure of plants throughout the 1970s, the city was left with few substantial economic alternatives.[[105]](#cite_note-Fuechtmann4143-105) The September 19, 1977, announcement of the closure of a large portion of [Youngstown Sheet and Tube](/wiki/Youngstown_Sheet_and_Tube "Youngstown Sheet and Tube"), an event still referred to as "Black Monday", is widely regarded as the death knell of the old area steel industry in Youngstown.[[47]](#cite_note-Bruno_1999,_pp._9–10-47) In the wake of the steel plant shutdowns, the community lost an estimated 40,000 manufacturing jobs, 400 satellite businesses, $414 million in personal income, and from 33 to 75 percent of the school tax revenues.[[106]](#cite_note-106) The Youngstown area has yet to fully recover from the loss of jobs in the steel sector.[[107]](#cite_note-107)

The blow dealt to the community's industrial economy in the 1970s was slightly mitigated by the auto production plants in the metropolitan area. In the late 1980s, the [Avanti](/wiki/Studebaker_Avanti "Studebaker Avanti"), an automobile with a fiberglass body originally designed by [Studebaker](/wiki/Studebaker "Studebaker") to compete with the [Corvette](/wiki/Chevrolet_Corvette "Chevrolet Corvette"), was manufactured in an industrial complex on Youngstown's Albert Street. This company moved away after just a few years.[[108]](#cite_note-Avanti-108) A mainstay of Youngstown's industrial economy had long been the [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors_Corporation "General Motors Corporation") [Lordstown Assembly](/wiki/Lordstown_Assembly "Lordstown Assembly"), which was the area's largest industrial employer in the decades following the decline of the steel industry.[[109]](#cite_note-GM-109) Once one of the nation's largest auto plants in terms of square feet, the Lordstown facility was home to production of the Chevrolet Impala, Vega, and [Cavalier](/wiki/Cavalier "Cavalier").[[109]](#cite_note-GM-109) The Lordstown Assembly was shuttered in March 2019 and is currently owned by [Foxconn](/wiki/Foxconn "Foxconn").[[110]](#cite_note-110)

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Powers_Auditorium_in_Youngstown.jpg/250px-Powers_Auditorium_in_Youngstown.jpg)](/wiki/File:Powers_Auditorium_in_Youngstown.jpg)

[Powers Auditorium](/wiki/Powers_Auditorium "Powers Auditorium") at DeYor Performing Arts Center

Despite the impact of regional economic decline, Youngstown offers an array of cultural resources. The [Youngstown Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Youngstown_Symphony_Orchestra "Youngstown Symphony Orchestra") has been based in the city since 1926.[[111]](#cite_note-d820-111) It performs at [Powers Auditorium](/wiki/Powers_Auditorium "Powers Auditorium"), a former [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros. "Warner Bros.") movie palace that serves as the area's primary music hall.[[112]](#cite_note-112)[[113]](#cite_note-nyt-03-19-30-113) This downtown landmark is one of five auditoriums within the city. Ford Recital Hall was built in 2006 as an addition to Powers Auditorium.

[Stambaugh Auditorium](/wiki/Stambaugh_Auditorium "Stambaugh Auditorium"), located on the city's north side, is an imposing neo-classical structure that has served for decades as a site for concerts and private events. The facility also hosts the Stambaugh Youth Concert Band.[[114]](#cite_note-stam-114) [Bruce Springsteen](/wiki/Bruce_Springsteen "Bruce Springsteen"), who sang about the decline of Youngstown's steel industry and its adverse effects on local workers in his ballad "[Youngstown](/wiki/Youngstown_(song) "Youngstown (song)")", played at Stambaugh Auditorium on January 12, 1996, as part of his solo [Ghost of Tom Joad Tour](/wiki/Ghost_of_Tom_Joad_Tour "Ghost of Tom Joad Tour").[[115]](#cite_note-115)

Youngstown's newest venue is the [Youngstown Foundation Amphitheatre](/wiki/Youngstown_Foundation_Amphitheatre "Youngstown Foundation Amphitheatre"), an outdoor venue opened in 2019 upon former industrial grounds downtown that hosts various musicians.[[116]](#cite_note-116) Artists also perform at the [Covelli Centre](/wiki/Covelli_Centre "Covelli Centre") arena downtown. Historically, one of the area's most popular attractions was [Idora Park](/wiki/Idora_Park_(Youngstown) "Idora Park (Youngstown)") in the [Idora neighborhood](/wiki/Fosterville "Fosterville") on Youngstown's south side. An urban [amusement park](/wiki/Amusement_park "Amusement park"), it operated from 1899 until it was closed after a large fire destroyed many of its premier rides in 1984.[[117]](#cite_note-nris-117)

### Theater

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Theater")]

The [Youngstown Playhouse](/wiki/Youngstown_Playhouse "Youngstown Playhouse"), Mahoning County's primary community theater, has served the area for more than 100 years, established in 1924, despite intermittent financial problems. Believed by some observers to be the nation's oldest continuously operating community theater, the Youngstown Playhouse was the only community theater in Ohio to ever receive major institutional support from the [Ohio Arts Council](/wiki/Ohio_Arts_Council "Ohio Arts Council").[[118]](#cite_note-118) The [Oakland Center for the Arts](/wiki/Oakland_Center_for_the_Arts "Oakland Center for the Arts"), formerly in the downtown area, was a well-known venue for locally produced plays before it closed in 2015 due to poor management.[[119]](#cite_note-119) In late 2016. the Oakland Center for the Arts was re-established with a new focus on youth and kids theatre.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

Several community theaters have risen in the area such as The Rustbelt Theater Company, The Hopewell (formally Victorian Players), and Millennial Theatre Company who are known for their regional premieres and larger theatrical experiences.

Well known theatrical personalities from the Youngstown area include comedic actor [Joe Flynn](/wiki/Joe_Flynn_(US_actor) "Joe Flynn (US actor)"),[[121]](#cite_note-vindy-yo-07-20-74-121) screen actress [Elizabeth Hartman](/wiki/Elizabeth_Hartman "Elizabeth Hartman"),[[122]](#cite_note-steely-yo-11-22-64-122) singer and Broadway performer [Maureen McGovern](/wiki/Maureen_McGovern "Maureen McGovern"),[[123]](#cite_note-123) and television and screen actor [Ed O'Neill](/wiki/Ed_O%27Neill "Ed O'Neill").[[124]](#cite_note-irish-in-y-town-124)

### Museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Museums")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Butler_Institute_of_American_Art_07.jpg/250px-Butler_Institute_of_American_Art_07.jpg)](/wiki/File:Butler_Institute_of_American_Art_07.jpg)

The [Butler Institute of American Art](/wiki/Butler_Institute_of_American_Art "Butler Institute of American Art") was the first museum in the country dedicated to American art.[[125]](#cite_note-thebutler-125)

The [Butler Institute of American Art](/wiki/Butler_Institute_of_American_Art "Butler Institute of American Art") is on the northeastern edge of the Youngstown State University campus. Established by industrialist [Joseph G. Butler Jr.](/wiki/Joseph_G._Butler_Jr. "Joseph G. Butler Jr."), in 1919, it was the first museum in the country dedicated to American art.[[125]](#cite_note-thebutler-125) Across the street from the Butler Institute stands the [McDonough Museum of Art](/wiki/McDonough_Museum_of_Art "McDonough Museum of Art"), YSU's University Art Museum and the Mahoning Valley's center for contemporary art. The McDonough, established in 1991, features changing exhibitions by regional, national and international artists and provides public access to the work of students, faculty and alumni from the Department of Art.[[126]](#cite_note-126) The Clarence R. Smith Mineral Museum, also on the YSU campus, is operated by the university's geology department and housed in a campus building.[[127]](#cite_note-127)

To the immediate north of YSU is the Arms Family Museum of Local History. The museum, housed in a 1905 mansion on the main artery of Wick Avenue, is managed by the Mahoning Valley Historical Society. Once the estate of a local industrialist, it maintains period rooms that showcase the household's original contents, including furnishings, art objects, and personal artifacts. The museum mounts rotating exhibits on topics related to local history.

The [Youngstown Historical Center of Industry and Labor](/wiki/Youngstown_Historical_Center_of_Industry_and_Labor "Youngstown Historical Center of Industry and Labor") sits south of the YSU campus. This museum, owned and operated by the [Ohio Historical Society](/wiki/Ohio_Historical_Society "Ohio Historical Society"), focuses on the Mahoning Valley's history of steel production.[[128]](#cite_note-128) Other museums include the Children's Museum of the Valley,[[129]](#cite_note-129) an interactive educational center in the downtown area, and the Davis Education and Recreation Center, a small museum that showcases the history of Youngstown's [Mill Creek Park](/wiki/Mill_Creek_Park "Mill Creek Park").[[130]](#cite_note-millcreek-130)

On the city's north side the Youngstown Steel Heritage Foundation is constructing the Tod Engine Heritage Park, featuring a collection of steel industry equipment and artifacts. The main exhibit is a 1914 William Tod Co. rolling mill steam engine that was built in Youngstown and used at the Youngstown Sheet and Tube Brier Hill Works. The Tod Engine is one of three remaining rolling mill engines in the United States and is a Mechanical and Materials Engineering Landmark.[[131]](#cite_note-131)

## Parks and recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Parks and recreation")]

Further information: [Mill Creek Park](/wiki/Mill_Creek_Park "Mill Creek Park") and [Lincoln Park (Youngstown, Ohio)](/wiki/Lincoln_Park_(Youngstown,_Ohio) "Lincoln Park (Youngstown, Ohio)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Mill_Creek_Park_Suspension_Bridge.jpg/250px-Mill_Creek_Park_Suspension_Bridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mill_Creek_Park_Suspension_Bridge.jpg)

[Mill Creek Park "Cinderella" suspension bridge](/wiki/Mill_Creek_Park_Suspension_Bridge "Mill Creek Park Suspension Bridge")

Youngstown's most popular resource is [Mill Creek Park](/wiki/Mill_Creek_Park "Mill Creek Park"), a five-mile (8 km)-long stretch of woodland along the eponymous Mill Creek. Mill Creek Park is the oldest park district in Ohio, established as a township park in 1891. The park's highlights include the restored 19th century Lanterman's Mill, the rock formations of Bear's Den, scores of nature trails, the [Fellows Riverside Gardens](/wiki/Fellows_Riverside_Gardens "Fellows Riverside Gardens") and Education Center, the ["Cinderella" suspension bridge](/wiki/Mill_Creek_Park_Suspension_Bridge "Mill Creek Park Suspension Bridge"), and two 18-hole [Donald Ross](/wiki/Donald_Ross_(golf_course_architect) "Donald Ross (golf course architect)") golf courses.[[132]](#cite_note-mvhs-132)[[133]](#cite_note-vindy-11-21-2007-133) Mill Creek Park encompasses approximately 2,600 acres (1,100 ha), 20 miles (32 km) of drives and 15 miles (24 km) of foot trails. Its attractions include gardens, streams, lakes, woodlands, meadows, and wildlife.

Fellows Riverside Gardens' lookout point offers visitors contrasting views of the area. From the south side, the canopied woodlands overlooking Lake Glacier are visible; from the north side, visitors are presented with a view of downtown Youngstown. The park features two 18-hole golf courses. The North Course is on rolling terrain, while the South Course features narrow, tree-lined fairways.[[134]](#cite_note-134) Other features include playgrounds, athletic fields, and picnic areas.

In 2005, Mill Creek Park was placed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places").[[135]](#cite_note-vind-yo-06-14-2006-135) A plaque commemorating this event is near a memorial statue of [Volney Rogers](/wiki/Volney_Rogers "Volney Rogers"), the Youngstown attorney who set aside land for the creation of Mill Creek Park.[[130]](#cite_note-millcreek-130)

A smaller recreational area called [Wick Park](/wiki/Wick_Park "Wick Park") is on the city's north side. Wick Park's periphery is lined with early 20th-century mansions built by the city's industrialists, business leaders, and professionals during Youngstown's boom years.[[136]](#cite_note-pellegrini-136) Stambaugh Auditorium, a popular venue for concerts and other public events, is near the park's southwestern edge.[[114]](#cite_note-stam-114) Another small recreational area called Crandall Park is also on the north side. Crandall Park is surrounded by landscaped homes, tree-lined streets, and walkable access to shopping and recreation.[[137]](#cite_note-137) Several cemeteries (notably historic Oak Hill Cemetery) and small recreational spaces are scattered throughout the city, including Homestead Park, John White Park, Lynn Park, Borts Pool and the Northside Pool.

Youngstown has enjoyed a long tradition of professional and semi-professional sports.[[138]](#cite_note-138) The [Youngstown Phantoms](/wiki/Youngstown_Phantoms "Youngstown Phantoms") of the [United States Hockey League](/wiki/United_States_Hockey_League "United States Hockey League") have played at the [Covelli Centre](/wiki/Covelli_Centre "Covelli Centre") since 2003. Amateur soccer club Mahoning Trumbull United SC has competed in the Northern Ohio Soccer League since 2023.[[139]](#cite_note-139) Amateur flat-track roller derby team [Youngstown Area Roller Derby](/wiki/Youngstown_Area_Roller_Derby "Youngstown Area Roller Derby") was established in 2008 and plays against other teams within the Women's Flat Track Derby Association.[[140]](#cite_note-140)

In earlier decades, the city produced scores of minor league baseball teams, including the [Youngstown Ohio Works](/wiki/Youngstown_Ohio_Works "Youngstown Ohio Works"), [Youngstown Champs](/wiki/Youngstown_Champs "Youngstown Champs"), [Youngstown Indians](/wiki/Youngstown_Indians "Youngstown Indians"), [Youngstown Steelmen](/wiki/Youngstown_Steelmen "Youngstown Steelmen"), [Youngstown Browns](/wiki/Youngstown_Browns "Youngstown Browns"), [Youngstown Gremlins](/wiki/Youngstown_Gremlins "Youngstown Gremlins"), and Youngstown Athletics. Local enthusiasm for baseball was such that the community hosted championship games of the National Amateur Baseball Federation throughout the 1930s and 1940s.[[141]](#cite_note-ward-141) The area's minor league baseball teams were supplemented by semi-professional football teams, including the [Youngstown Patricians](/wiki/Youngstown_Patricians "Youngstown Patricians"), who won the 1915 championship of the informal "[Ohio League](/wiki/Ohio_League "Ohio League")" (a direct predecessor to the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League")),[[142]](#cite_note-142) and the [Youngstown Hardhats](/wiki/Youngstown_Hardhats "Youngstown Hardhats"), who competed in the Middle Atlantic Football League in the 1970s and early 1980s. For three seasons, Youngstown was home to the [Mahoning Valley Thunder](/wiki/Mahoning_Valley_Thunder "Mahoning Valley Thunder") of the now-defunct [af2](/wiki/Af2 "Af2"), the minor league for the [Arena Football League](/wiki/Arena_Football_League_(1987%E2%80%932008) "Arena Football League (1987–2008)") until 2009 when the franchise ceased operations.[[143]](#cite_note-vind-yo-03-31-2007-143)[[144]](#cite_note-144) In 2005, the Ohio Red Bulls semi-pro football team of the United States Football Association won their first championship.[[145]](#cite_note-145) Local minor league basketball teams included the [Youngstown Pride](/wiki/Youngstown_Pride_(basketball_team) "Youngstown Pride (basketball team)") of the [WBA](/wiki/World_Basketball_League "World Basketball League") from 1987 to 1992, the [Youngstown Hawks](/wiki/Youngstown_Hawks "Youngstown Hawks") of the [IBA](/wiki/International_Basketball_Association "International Basketball Association") in 1999, and the [Mahoning Valley Wildcats](/wiki/Mahoning_Valley_Wildcats "Mahoning Valley Wildcats") of the [IBL](/wiki/International_Basketball_League_(2005-) "International Basketball League (2005-)") in 2005. The [Youngstown SteelHounds](/wiki/Youngstown_SteelHounds "Youngstown SteelHounds") hockey team played in the [Central Hockey League](/wiki/Central_Hockey_League "Central Hockey League") from 2005 until 2008.

### Youngstown State Penguins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Youngstown State Penguins")]

The community has a lengthy tradition of collegiate sports. The [Youngstown State Penguins](/wiki/Youngstown_State_Penguins "Youngstown State Penguins") compete in the [Missouri Valley Football Conference](/wiki/Missouri_Valley_Football_Conference "Missouri Valley Football Conference"). The Penguins, noted participants in FCS (I-AA) football, play their games at [Stambaugh Stadium](/wiki/Stambaugh_Stadium "Stambaugh Stadium") and enjoy one of the more supportive fan bases. All other YSU athletic teams compete in the [Horizon League](/wiki/Horizon_League "Horizon League"), which does not sponsor football. The Youngstown State men and women's basketball teams hold their games at Youngstown State's [Beeghly Center](/wiki/Beeghly_Center "Beeghly Center"). The teams average about 2,500 fans per game, a number that rose with a new style of play under former head coach [Jerry Slocum](/wiki/Jerry_Slocum_(basketball) "Jerry Slocum (basketball)"). In addition, the YSU baseball and softball teams have enjoyed local support and success. The baseball team reached the NCAA super-regionals in 2005, and the softball team did so in 2006.[[155]](#cite_note-ysusports-155)

## Government and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Government and politics")]

See also: [List of mayors of Youngstown, Ohio](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Youngstown,_Ohio "List of mayors of Youngstown, Ohio")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Mahoning_County_Courthouse.jpg/250px-Mahoning_County_Courthouse.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mahoning_County_Courthouse.jpg)

[Mahoning County](/wiki/Mahoning_County "Mahoning County") Courthouse

Youngstown is governed by a mayor who is elected every four years. Youngstown's mayor is Derrick McDowell, who was elected in 2025.[[156]](#cite_note-j617-156) The city has tended to elect [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") mayors since the late 1920s because the local unions supported Democratic candidates for office.[[157]](#cite_note-157) Residents elect an eight-member city council composed of representatives of the city's seven wards and a council president. The council traditionally meets every first and third Wednesday of the month.[[158]](#cite_note-158) Meanwhile, the board of control, chaired by the mayor, oversees contracts for public projects within the municipal limits.[[159]](#cite_note-159)

In the [Ohio General Assembly](/wiki/Ohio_General_Assembly "Ohio General Assembly"), Youngstown is located in the [59th State Representative District](/wiki/Ohio_House_of_Representatives "Ohio House of Representatives"), represented by Democrat [Lauren McNally](/wiki/Lauren_McNally "Lauren McNally"),[[160]](#cite_note-160) and in the [33rd Senate District](/wiki/Ohio_Senate "Ohio Senate"), represented by Republican [Alessandro Cutrona](/wiki/Alessandro_Cutrona "Alessandro Cutrona").[[161]](#cite_note-161) Federally, Youngstown has been located in [Ohio's 6th congressional district](/wiki/Ohio%27s_6th_congressional_district "Ohio's 6th congressional district") since 2023 after being redistricted.[[162]](#cite_note-162) The [Ohio Seventh District Court of Appeals](/wiki/Ohio_Seventh_District_Court_of_Appeals "Ohio Seventh District Court of Appeals") is based in the city, as is one of four courthouses of the [United States District Court for the Northern District of Ohio](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_Northern_District_of_Ohio "United States District Court for the Northern District of Ohio").[[163]](#cite_note-y564-163)

Like many urban areas in the U.S., Youngstown is a Democratic stronghold, although the remainder of Mahoning County has been trending to the right in recent elections.[[164]](#cite_note-164) Youngstown has become a political backdrop for both Democrats and Republicans who go to the area to campaign on economic development and jobs.[[165]](#cite_note-165)[[166]](#cite_note-166) Circa 1972 to 2016,[[167]](#cite_note-167) the majority of voters in Mahoning County and Youngstown chose Democratic Party candidates in U.S. presidential elections. [Tex Fischer](/wiki/Tex_Fischer "Tex Fischer") stated that during the [2012 U.S. presidential election](/wiki/2012_U.S._presidential_election "2012 U.S. presidential election"), area residents perceived [Mitt Romney](/wiki/Mitt_Romney "Mitt Romney") as being inauthentic, which contributed to his loss.[[168]](#cite_note-Gumbel-168)

However, in the [2020 U.S. presidential election](/wiki/2020_U.S._presidential_election "2020 U.S. presidential election"), the majority in Mahoning County selected [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump"). Andrew Gumbell of *[The Observer](/wiki/The_Observer "The Observer")* stated that Trump gained popularity from 2017 to 2020 even though the Youngstown area economy declined in the same period; Trump in 2017 made statements saying that he would revive the area economically. In the [2024 U.S. presidential election](/wiki/2024_U.S._presidential_election "2024 U.S. presidential election"), Trump won the county by 13 points. Gumbell cited "disillusioned working-class voters" and their feelings for the rising popularity of Trump in the area; according to Gumbell, the voters perceive all politicians to be corrupt, but Trump to be honest about being a corrupt person.[[168]](#cite_note-Gumbel-168) Gumbell added that the voters believe that Trump would abolish a system that disadvantages them, but that the majority of area voters do not believe that, in Gumbell's words, that Trump will "fix everything or believe him when he says he will."[[168]](#cite_note-Gumbel-168)

### Crime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Crime")]

Crime has been a lingering problem in many of the [Rust Belt](/wiki/Rust_Belt "Rust Belt")'s big and small urban communities, hampering economic recovery.[[169]](#cite_note-169) In the late 1950s and early 1960s, Youngstown was nationally identified with gangland slayings often committed with car bombs.[[170]](#cite_note-heltzel-170) The town gained the nicknames "Murdertown, USA" and "Bomb City, USA," while the phrase "Youngstown tune-up" became a nationally popular slang term for car-bomb assassination.[[171]](#cite_note-grann-171) The image of Youngstown's association with crime was reinforced by the construction of prisons inside the metropolitan area.[[172]](#cite_note-clines-172) As of 2012, three adult correctional facilities continue to operate within city limits: the Mahoning County Justice Center[[173]](#cite_note-173) the [Northeast Ohio Correctional Center](/wiki/Northeast_Ohio_Correctional_Center "Northeast Ohio Correctional Center"),[[174]](#cite_note-174) and the [Ohio State Penitentiary](/wiki/Ohio_State_Penitentiary "Ohio State Penitentiary").[[175]](#cite_note-175)

For decades, Youngstown was a haven for organized crime, and related corruption was ingrained into the fabric of its society. A 2000 publication in *[The New Republic](/wiki/The_New_Republic "The New Republic")* listed a "chief of police, the outgoing prosecutor, the sheriff, the county engineer, members of the local police force, a city law director, several defense attorneys, politicians, judges, and a former assistant U.S. attorney" as controlled by the [Mob](/wiki/American_Mafia "American Mafia").[[176]](#cite_note-176) The city accelerated measures to limit the influence of organized crime upon all sectors of municipal life. In 2006, Youngstown was ranked by [Morgan Quitno Press](/wiki/Morgan_Quitno_Press "Morgan Quitno Press"), a Kansas-based publishing and research company, as the 9th most dangerous city in the United States.[[177]](#cite_note-177) After [The Saturday Evening Post](/wiki/The_Saturday_Evening_Post "The Saturday Evening Post") framed Youngstown as "Crimetown U.S.A.", there was an interest by many to create documentaries or podcasts to get in-depth information about the corruption unfolding in the city. Released in July 2022, Marc Smerling released a podcast titled "Crooked City" to share some of those stories.[[178]](#cite_note-178)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Youngstown_State_University_clock_tower_as_seen_from_Moser_Hall.jpg/250px-Youngstown_State_University_clock_tower_as_seen_from_Moser_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Youngstown_State_University_clock_tower_as_seen_from_Moser_Hall.jpg)

The campus of [Youngstown State University](/wiki/Youngstown_State_University "Youngstown State University") is located north of [downtown Youngstown](/wiki/Downtown_Youngstown "Downtown Youngstown").

Youngstown is served by the [Public Library of Youngstown and Mahoning County](/wiki/Public_Library_of_Youngstown_and_Mahoning_County "Public Library of Youngstown and Mahoning County") system, with libraries located in the Downtown (Main), Brownlee Woods, East High, Newport, and Schenley (Michael Kusalaba) neighborhoods.[[179]](#cite_note-179)

### Primary and secondary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Primary and secondary")]

The [Youngstown City School District](/wiki/Youngstown_City_School_District "Youngstown City School District") manages public education within the city and covers almost all of the city limits.[[180]](#cite_note-180)[[181]](#cite_note-TrumbullCoSDMap2020-181) Since 2015, the state government has overseen the district's operation due to district mismanagement.[[182]](#cite_note-182) The district's high school graduation rate has improved since the takeover, from 65% in 2015 to 88% in 2020.[[183]](#cite_note-183)[[184]](#cite_note-184) YCSD currently operates six [elementary schools](/wiki/Elementary_schools "Elementary schools"), three [middle schools](/wiki/Middle_schools "Middle schools"), and three [high schools](/wiki/High_schools "High schools"), as well as one alternative school and one [technical school](/wiki/Technical_school "Technical school").[[185]](#cite_note-185) The district extensively built new schools throughout the late 2000s, and sold many of its older buildings to local private schools.[[186]](#cite_note-186)

At one time, the school district had eight dedicated public high schools (including now-defunct [North](/wiki/North_High_School_(Youngstown,_Ohio) "North High School (Youngstown, Ohio)"), [Rayen](/wiki/Rayen_High_School "Rayen High School"), [South](/wiki/South_High_School_(Youngstown,_Ohio) "South High School (Youngstown, Ohio)"), and [Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson_High_School_(Youngstown,_Ohio) "Woodrow Wilson High School (Youngstown, Ohio)")), but mergers since the 1990s have resulted in four secondary schools: [Chaney High School](/wiki/Chaney_High_School "Chaney High School"), Choffin Career and Technical Center, [East High School](/wiki/East_High_School_(Youngstown,_Ohio) "East High School (Youngstown, Ohio)"), and Youngstown Rayen Early College.[[187]](#cite_note-vindy-yo-03-14-07-187) The [early college](/wiki/Early_college "Early college") program, in cooperation with Youngstown State University, enables middle and high school students to attend classes both on campus and at YCSD schools and earn college credit.[[188]](#cite_note-188)

The [Diocese of Youngstown](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Youngstown "Roman Catholic Diocese of Youngstown") once oversaw more than 20 schools within the city. As a result of dwindling enrollment, only four [Catholic schools](/wiki/Catholic_school "Catholic school") now operate within Youngstown proper.[[189]](#cite_note-gwin-189) These include two primary schools—St. Christine's School and [St. Joseph the Provider School](http://stjosephtheproviderschool.com/)—and two secondary schools, [Cardinal Mooney High School](/wiki/Cardinal_Mooney_High_School_(Ohio) "Cardinal Mooney High School (Ohio)") and [Ursuline High School](/wiki/Ursuline_High_School_(Youngstown,_Ohio) "Ursuline High School (Youngstown, Ohio)"). Several additional Catholic schools operate in the region which accept Youngstown students.

Youngstown hosts a small number of private schools. These include Valley Christian School, a [nondenominational](/wiki/Christian_school "Christian school") K-12 school; Akiva Academy, a progressive K–8 school in the Jewish Community Center; and the [Montessori](/wiki/Montessori "Montessori") School of the Mahoning Valley, which offers alternative learning environments for students ranging from preschool to eighth grade.[[190]](#cite_note-190) There are also various smaller, K-8 charter academies in the city, such as the Stambaugh Charter Academy and South Side Academy.

### Higher education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Higher education")]

[Youngstown State University](/wiki/Youngstown_State_University "Youngstown State University"), the primary institution of higher learning in the Youngstown metropolitan area, traces its origins to a local [YMCA](/wiki/YMCA "YMCA") program that began offering college-level courses in 1908.[[191]](#cite_note-vindy-10-14-07-191) YSU joined the Ohio system of higher education in 1967.[[191]](#cite_note-vindy-10-14-07-191) The university has an enrollment of about 11,000 undergraduate and graduate students within its seven colleges.[[88]](#cite_note-headcount-88)[[192]](#cite_note-192) The campus is just north of the city's downtown and south of Youngstown's historic Fifth Avenue district, a neighborhood of [Tudor](/wiki/Tudor_style_architecture "Tudor style architecture")-, [Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_architecture "Victorian architecture")-, and [Spanish Colonial Revival](/wiki/Spanish_Colonial_Revival_Style_architecture "Spanish Colonial Revival Style architecture")-style homes.[[136]](#cite_note-pellegrini-136)

[Eastern Gateway Community College](/wiki/Eastern_Gateway_Community_College "Eastern Gateway Community College") operated a campus in downtown Youngstown from 2009 to 2024.[[193]](#cite_note-193)

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Media")]

Main article: [Media in Youngstown, Ohio](/wiki/Media_in_Youngstown,_Ohio "Media in Youngstown, Ohio")

### Print

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Print")]

*[The Vindicator](/wiki/The_Vindicator "The Vindicator")* is the sole daily newspaper in the city, currently published as a zoned edition of [Warren's](/wiki/Warren,_Ohio "Warren, Ohio") *[Tribune Chronicle](/wiki/Tribune_Chronicle "Tribune Chronicle")* in [broadsheet](/wiki/Broadsheet "Broadsheet"). It formerly competed with the Warren-based paper, and the [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon,_Ohio "Lisbon, Ohio")-based *Morning Journal*, although they primarily covered their respective counties, with limited coverage of Mahoning County and Youngstown, until in June 2019 it was announced that *The Vindicator* would cease publication by mid-August of the same year.[[194]](#cite_note-194) Although this newspaper carries the name of the old *Vindicator*,[[195]](#cite_note-195) its scope is comparatively limited, with the majority of previous *Vindicator* journalists not being carried over to the new edition.[[196]](#cite_note-196)

Other newspapers that print in Youngstown include bi-monthly *The Business Journal*, *The Metro Monthly*, and the bi-weekly *The Jambar*, published by the students of Youngstown State University on Tuesdays and Thursdays while classes are in session.

### TV

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Youngstown,_Ohio&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: TV")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/WKBN-TV.JPG/250px-WKBN-TV.JPG)](/wiki/File:WKBN-TV.JPG)

[WKBN-TV](/wiki/WKBN-TV "WKBN-TV") studio

With 273,480 television households, the Youngstown market is the nation's 106th largest, according to [Nielsen Media Research](/wiki/Nielsen_Media_Research "Nielsen Media Research").[[197]](#cite_note-197)

The market is served is served by four full power television stations, all of which are [UHF](/wiki/Ultra_high_frequency "Ultra high frequency") stations.[[198]](#cite_note-radiostationworld-198) The Youngstown market's stations include: [WFMJ-TV](/wiki/WFMJ-TV "WFMJ-TV") (channel 21, [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC"), with [The CW](/wiki/The_CW_Television_Network "The CW Television Network") channel 21.2 under the [WBCB](/wiki/WFMJ-DT2 "WFMJ-DT2") call letters), [WKBN-TV](/wiki/WKBN-TV "WKBN-TV") (channel 27, [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS")), [WYTV](/wiki/WYTV "WYTV") (channel 33, [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company"), with [MNTV](/wiki/My_Network_TV "My Network TV") on [33.2](/wiki/WYTV-DT2 "WYTV-DT2")), and [WNEO](/wiki/Western_Reserve_Public_Media "Western Reserve Public Media") channel 45 ([PBS](/wiki/Public_Broadcasting_Service "Public Broadcasting Service")).

Low power station [WYFX-LD](/wiki/WYFX-LD "WYFX-LD") channel 62 serves as Youngstown's [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company") affiliate, and is simulcast on WKBN 27.2.
